original_abstract: This article explores the missional potential inherent in the two-volume narrative of Ezra-Nehemiah, positing that the written Word of God acts as a transformative force within society when mediated through the church. Central to this argument is the conviction that authentic social reconstruction must be preceded by spiritual renewal. By employing historical, canonical, and exegetical methodologies, the study establishes a rigorous foundation for missiological reflection based on biblical scholarship. The investigation concludes by formulating key missional principles intended to serve as strategic guidelines for the development of a contemporary and contextually relevant theology of mission.
humanized_abstract: One of the largest obstacles for mission studies today is determining where within Scripture one is to begin in trying to identify and explore the missional potential of the text. Because each biblical book has a unique structure and form, engaging a book as a whole can sometimes prove challenging for mission studies. One way to address this challenge is to observe that a written Word of God will always be a source of renewal and restoration to any given culture through the ministry of the church. Given the complex nature of the social challenge before the Western world, there is probably no way to restore the cultures of the Western world without significant spiritual renewal. It is only by looking at Scripture through a proper lens of historical, canonical, and exegetical investigation that a church engaged in mission can then apply these findings to the mission field in order to form mission strategies appropriate to their context. The present study works its way through each book in a sequential fashion exploring Ezra-Nehemiah in its entirety through the methods and tools mentioned above in order to finally identify key missional principles to which the church may seek to adapt in order to develop a contextual missions theology based on a holistic Scriptural understanding of mission.
